front: Poem: Part II addresses Moloch, a 'sphinx of cement and aluminum' whose attributes include 'whose fate is a cloud of sexless hydrogen' and 'whose name is the Mind!'
Poem: Part III repeatedly tells Carl Solomon 'I'm with you in Rockland'
Poem: a lengthy footnote proclaims 'the world is holy! the soul is holy!'; includes the line about 'saintly motorcyclists' that triggered an obscenity trial
Poem: first read at the Six Gallery Reading in October 1955; City Lights Books published it and Lawrence Ferlinghetti was arrested for obscenity
Poem: originally dedicated to Lucien Carr, who formulated the 'New Vision' of the Beat poets and went to jail for the murder of David Kammerer
Poem: Walt Whitman, a 'childless, lonely old grubber,' asks 'Who killed the pork chops? What price bananas? Are you my angel?' while eyeing grocery boys
Poem: the speaker asks Garcia Lorca 'what were you doing down by the watermelons?' in a supermarket
Poem: Whitman ends the poem as a 'lonely old courage-teacher' watching 'the boat disappear on the black waters of Lethe'
Poem: the speaker is obsessed with Time Magazine, describes being sold garbanzos at a Communist meeting at age seven, and asks 'when will you send your eggs to India?'
Poem: opens 'I've given you all and now I'm nothing'; the speaker compares his strophes to Henry Ford's automobiles
Poem: elegy for the poet's mother Naomi, opens 'Strange now to think of you, gone without corsets & eyes'; she is blessed 'in tears, in fears, in sickness, in Hospitals, in Death'
Poem: the mother has a 'Communist Party and a broken stocking' and a 'fear of Hitler'; in a letter she writes 'I have the key' and 'the key is in the bars, in the sunlight'
Poem: the speaker finds a 'skeleton thick' sunflower in a railyard with Jack Kerouac and has a Blakean revelation that 'we're not our skin of grime'
Author: had an auditory hallucination of William Blake's voice in 1948; helped Chogyam Trungpa establish the Naropa Institute; practiced Buddhism
Author: invented the 'American Sentence,' a single line of 17 syllables; translated Basho's frog haiku ending with 'Kerplunk!'
Author: appeared as guest vocalist on The Clash's 'Ghetto Defendant,' reciting a Buddhist sutra
